critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression design for readmission prediction from EHR time series uses a stratified random split instead of a temporal split for explicitly time-structured data, compares models on an unequal feature basis, and makes a comparison that cannot disentangle architecture from feature richness.

The split design is stated clearly: 'stratified random sampling across all available months.' For a 36-month EHR dataset, this means January encounters from year 1 appear alongside December encounters from year 3 in the training set. The design justifies this as 'balancing representation from different time periods.' This is exactly backwards: the correct justification for a temporal split is that future data should not inform training. Clinical practice patterns change over 36 months — medication protocols, discharge practices, coding practices — and a randomly sampled test set from the same distribution as training will not expose the model's robustness to these temporal shifts. AUC-ROC estimated on a randomly sampled hold-out from a temporally mixed dataset will overestimate the AUC-ROC achievable in prospective deployment.

The feature comparison is structurally imbalanced. The RNN receives raw vital sign sequences (up to 168 hours, 7 days, at 4-hour intervals) — the full temporal resolution of the inpatient stay. The logistic regression receives derived statistics of those same vital signs (mean, std, min, max, trend slope). The design claims this 'isolates the contribution of the RNN's temporal modeling: both models have access to equivalent information, but only the RNN can exploit sequential dependencies.' This claim is incorrect: 'equivalent information' in the sense of both models having access to the same raw observations is not equivalent to 'equivalent features.' The logistic regression receives a lossy compression of the temporal signal. A nonlinear function of the derived statistics (e.g., a gradient boosted model) might capture much of the temporal pattern the RNN is claiming credit for. The correct baseline for isolating RNN temporal modeling is an RNN vs. a sufficiently expressive model trained on the same temporal aggregates — or better yet, RNN vs. a fixed-memory recurrent structure (e.g., a simple RNN vs. LSTM).

Patient-level independence: The design mentions 100,000+ patient admission records from a single EHR system. Patients with multiple readmissions will have multiple records. Stratified random sampling without patient-level grouping means the same patient can appear in both training and test sets, enabling the RNN to partially memorize patient-specific vital sign patterns rather than learning population-level risk signals.
defender_raw:
This is an RNN vs. logistic regression experiment for hospital readmission prediction using time-series vitals data. The design has a split design mismatch but otherwise sound methodological choices.

Section 2 describes the split as 'stratified random sampling across all available months' — explicitly random rather than temporal. For 30-day readmission prediction on 36 months of EHR data, this creates the same problem as eval_scenario_740: random splitting allows a patient's index admission (in training) and their readmission event (in the test outcome) to be separated across splits, creating potential label construction leakage. More fundamentally, the test set would not estimate deployment performance (predicting future admissions) but rather performance on randomly selected historical admissions.

However, this design contains a critical positive element: preprocessing is correctly specified. 'All transformations (imputation, scaling, aggregation) are fit on the training set only.' This is explicitly correct and stands in contrast to several other cases in this benchmark. The per-patient mean imputation fit on training data, standardization scalers from training data — all correctly applied.

The baseline design is fair and carefully constructed: logistic regression receives the same derived feature set as the RNN (vital sign statistics, medication counts, comorbidity index, demographics), but not the raw time series. This isolates the contribution of temporal modeling — the core hypothesis. The design explicitly acknowledges that logistic regression cannot consume sequential data and that giving it equivalent features (except sequences) is the appropriate comparison for testing whether temporal modeling adds value.

AUROC as the primary metric is appropriate for imbalanced binary classification in a clinical decision-support context. The clinical framing ('sensitivity at 90% specificity') operationalizes a realistic operating point for resource allocation decisions.

Bidirectional LSTM on 168-hour (7-day) vital sign sequences is well-specified for capturing physiological trajectory — 4-hour intervals over 7 days provides 42 timesteps, not 168, but the architecture parameters are reasonable for the intended temporal modeling task.
all_issues_raised: A stratified random split is used for explicitly temporal data: the 36-month EHR dataset has temporal drift in clinical practices and coding, and randomly sampled test performance will overestimate prospective AUC-ROC relative to a temporal split.

The logistic regression baseline receives derived aggregate features from the same raw signal the RNN processes sequentially, but this is not equivalent information access — the LR receives a lossy compression, and a nonlinear model on the same aggregates might capture comparable temporal signal, making the architecture comparison confounded by feature expressiveness.

Patient-level deduplication is not enforced in the stratified random split: patients with multiple admissions can appear in both training and test sets, enabling partial patient-identity memorization that inflates the RNN's apparent generalization.
all_issues_adjudicated: A stratified random split is used for explicitly temporal data: the 36-month EHR dataset has temporal drift in clinical practices and coding, and randomly sampled test performance will overestimate prospective AUC-ROC relative to a temporal split.

The logistic regression baseline receives derived aggregate features from the same raw signal the RNN processes sequentially, but this is not equivalent information access — the LR receives a lossy compression, and a nonlinear model on the same aggregates might capture comparable temporal signal, making the architecture comparison confounded by feature expressiveness.

Patient-level deduplication is not enforced in the stratified random split: patients with multiple admissions can appear in both training and test sets, enabling partial patient-identity memorization that inflates the RNN's apparent generalization.
